```typescript
    mockHasWord.mockReturnValue(true);
    mockAnalyze.mockReturnValue({
      phones: 'p-uh1 t-ih-ng',
      syllables: 'p-uh/t-ih-ng',
      stresses: '1/0',
      pos: 'vbg',
    });

    const result = await generateBreakdown('putting');

    expect(result.ritaKnown).toBe(true);
    expect(result.phonemes).toEqual(['p', 'ʊ', 't', 'ɪ', 'ŋ']);
    expect(result.ipa).toBe('pʊtɪŋ');
  });

  it('derives letter-space syllables from phoneme-space syllables', async () => {
    mockHasWord.mockReturnValue(true);
    mockAnalyze.mockReturnValue({
      phones: 'p-uh1 t-ih-ng',
      syllables: 'p-uh/t-ih-ng',
      stresses: '1/0',
      pos: 'vbg',
    });

    const result = await generateBreakdown('putting');

    expect(result.syllables.join('')).toBe('putting');
    expect(result.syllables).toHaveLength(2);
  });

  it('lowercases and trims the word before calling Rita', async () => {
    mockHasWord.mockReturnValue(true);
    mockAnalyze.mockReturnValue({
      phones: 'k-ae1 t',
      syllables: 'k-ae-t',
      stresses: '1',
      pos: 'nn',
    });

    await generateBreakdown('  CAT  ');

    expect(mockHasWord).toHaveBeenCalledWith('cat');
    expect(mockAnalyze).toHaveBeenCalledWith('cat', { silent: true });
  });

  it('uses the LTS fallback so rita-unknown words still get phonemes and syllables', async () => {
    // hasWord returns false (dictionary miss) but analyze's
    // letter-to-sound engine still produces a guess.
    mockHasWord.mockReturnValue(false);
    mockAnalyze.mockReturnValue({
      phones: 'p-r-ow1 th-ah s-ih-s',
      syllables: 'p-r-ow/th-ah/s-ih-s',
      stresses: '1/0/0',
      pos: 'nn',
    });

    const result = await generateBreakdown('prothesis');

    expect(result.ritaKnown).toBe(false);
    expect(result.phonemes.length).toBeGreaterThan(0);
    expect(result.syllables.length).toBeGreaterThan(0);
    expect(result.ipa).not.toBe('');
    expect(result.syllables.join('')).toBe('prothesis');
  });

  it('returns an empty breakdown only when analyze produces no phones', async () => {
    mockHasWord.mockReturnValue(false);
    mockAnalyze.mockReturnValue({
      phones: '',
      syllables: '',
      stresses: '',
      pos: '',
    });

    const result = await generateBreakdown('xzqxzq');

    expect(result.phonemes).toEqual([]);
    expect(result.syllables).toEqual([]);
    expect(result.ipa).toBe('');
    expect(result.ritaKnown).toBe(false);
  });

  it('falls back to an empty breakdown when analyze throws', async () => {
    mockHasWord.mockReturnValue(false);
    mockAnalyze.mockImplementation(() => {
      throw new Error('boom');
    });

    const result = await generateBreakdown('prothesis');

    expect(result.phonemes).toEqual([]);
    expect(result.syllables).toEqual([]);
  });
});
```

```typescript
import { arpabetStringToIpa } from './arpabet-to-ipa';

export interface Breakdown {
  word: string;
  ipa: string;
  syllables: string[];
  phonemes: string[];
  /**
   * True iff the word is in RiTa's dictionary. When false, the
   * phonemes and syllables (if any) come from RiTa's letter-to-sound
   * fallback — usable but guessed. The authoring UI keeps a
   * dictionary.com banner for that case so teachers can verify.
   */
  ritaKnown: boolean;
}

const splitSyllablesByPhonemeBoundary = (
  word: string,
  phonemes: string[],
  phonemeSyllables: string[],
): string[] => {
  if (phonemeSyllables.length <= 1) return [word];

  const lettersPerPhoneme = word.length / phonemes.length;
  const boundaries: number[] = [];
  let acc = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < phonemeSyllables.length - 1; i += 1) {
    acc += phonemeSyllables[i]!.split('-').filter(Boolean).length;
    boundaries.push(Math.round(acc * lettersPerPhoneme));
  }

  const out: string[] = [];
  let prev = 0;
  for (const b of boundaries) {
    const cut = Math.min(Math.max(b, prev + 1), word.length - 1);
    out.push(word.slice(prev, cut));
    prev = cut;
  }
  out.push(word.slice(prev));
  return out.filter((s) => s.length > 0);
};

interface RitaAnalysis {
  phones?: string;
  syllables?: string;
  stresses?: string;
  pos?: string;
}

export const generateBreakdown = async (
  rawWord: string,
): Promise<Breakdown> => {
  const word = rawWord.trim().toLowerCase();
  const { RiTa } = await import('rita');
  const ritaKnown = RiTa.hasWord(word);

  // RiTa.analyze gives us phones + syllables in one call and
  // returns LTS-derived guesses for words outside the dictionary
  // (the `hasWord` miss case). Using it unconditionally means
  // "prothesis" etc. still come back with usable data — marked
  // `ritaKnown: false` so the UI can warn the guess needs review.
  let phonemes: string[] = [];
  let phonemeSyllables: string[] = [];
  try {
    const analysis = (RiTa.analyze(word, { silent: true }) ??
      {}) as RitaAnalysis;
    const phonesStr = analysis.phones ?? '';
    const syllStr = analysis.syllables ?? '';
    if (phonesStr) phonemes = arpabetStringToIpa(phonesStr);
    if (syllStr) phonemeSyllables = syllStr.split('/');
  } catch (error) {
    // arpabetStringToIpa throws on unknown tokens; treat as "no
    // alignment available" and let the caller bootstrap. Surface
    // the error for debugging.
    console.error('[authoring] RiTa.analyze parse failed:', error);
  }

  if (phonemes.length === 0) {
    return {
      word,
      ipa: '',
      syllables: [],
      phonemes: [],
      ritaKnown,
    };
  }

  const syllables = splitSyllablesByPhonemeBoundary(
    word,
    phonemes,
    phonemeSyllables,
  );

  return {
    word,
    ipa: phonemes.join(''),
    syllables,
    phonemes,
    ritaKnown,
  };
};

```

Commit engine + panel changes

```bash
git add src/data/words/authoring/engine.ts src/data/words/authoring/engine.test.ts src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.tsx && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(word-authoring): use RiTa.analyze so prothesis and friends get LTS-derived alignment

Before: `generateBreakdown` gated every RitaJS call on `RiTa.hasWord`.
When the word wasn't in the dictionary the function short-circuited
with empty phonemes/syllables, which flowed through to the authoring
panel as "no chips, no syllable display" — the bootstrap fallback
added earlier was the only affordance, and users had to split a single
full-word chip one letter at a time.

But RitaJS's letter-to-sound (LTS) engine does produce a usable guess
for most inputs outside the dictionary. `RiTa.analyze(word)` returns
`{phones, syllables, stresses, pos}` via LTS when `hasWord` is false,
and we were throwing that away.

- `generateBreakdown` now uses `RiTa.analyze` as the single data
  source for phones + syllables. `hasWord` stays for the `ritaKnown`
  flag so the UI can still warn that the alignment is guessed.
- Parse errors from `arpabetStringToIpa` (e.g. exotic LTS tokens) and
  any thrown analysis are caught and surfaced via console.error;
  bootstrap still handles the fallback case.
- AuthoringPanel drops `b.ritaKnown` from the chip-alignment gate —
  any non-empty phonemes array feeds `align`. The bootstrap single
  chip now only fires when there is literally nothing to align
  (empty word, or analyze threw).
- Banner re-worded to acknowledge that data exists and is a guess:
  "<word> isn't in RitaJS's dictionary — the phonemes and syllables
  below are letter-to-sound guesses. Please confirm in a dictionary
  before saving → dictionary.com". Prior copy implied no data was
  available, which is no longer true.

TDD: engine tests rewritten around `RiTa.analyze` (mock replaces the
old `phones`/`syllables` pair). New cases: LTS fallback for
rita-unknown words, empty-phones fallback, analyze-throws fallback.
AuthoringPanel tests still pass as-is — the mock engine gates on
word identity not `hasWord`, so the bootstrap path it exercises
remains the "phones empty" branch.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5
```

Commit syllables editor

```bash
git add src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.tsx src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.test.tsx && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(word-authoring): syllables become editable — state, bootstrap, split, delete

Syllables were display-only before: read directly from
breakdown.syllables and rendered as <span>s. For rita-unknown words
(where the engine returned no alignment) the row was empty; for
rita-known words the user couldn't fix a mis-split. Parallel bug to
the chip row we just solved.

- `useDebouncedBreakdown` now owns a `syllables: string[]` state
  alongside chips. Seeded from `initialDraft.syllables` in edit mode;
  otherwise filled from the engine response or — when that's empty
  and the word has text — bootstrapped to `[word]` so the user has
  something to split.
- `splitSyllable(i)` takes the last letter of syllable `i` into a
  new entry at `i + 1`; disabled when the syllable has one letter.
- `deleteSyllable(i)` removes the syllable and merges its letters
  into a neighbour (previous by default, next when `i === 0`);
  disabled when only one syllable remains. Both operations preserve
  the invariant `syllables.join('') === word`.
- Syllable row renders clickable buttons with the same
  selected-ring affordance the chip row uses, plus a small editor
  card below with ✂ Split / 🗑 Delete (mirrors the chip editor
  layout so the two interactions feel identical).
- Save path now passes the stateful `syllables` to
  `draftStore.saveDraft` / `updateDraft` instead of
  `breakdown?.syllables`, so user edits persist.

TDD: four failing tests covered split (count grows + join invariant),
delete (count shrinks + letters merge), bootstrap (single full-word
syllable for rita-unknown words, splittable), and the only-one
disable guard.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5
```

```text
1	import type { CurriculumEntry, Grapheme } from '../types';
2	
3	export interface AlignedGrapheme extends Grapheme {
4	  confidence: number;
5	}
6	
7	const ausLoaders = import.meta.glob<{ default: CurriculumEntry[] }>(
8	  '../curriculum/aus/level*.json',
9	  { eager: true },
10	);
11	
12	const SPLIT_DIGRAPHS = ['a_e', 'i_e', 'o_e', 'u_e', 'e_e'] as const;
13	
14	interface Corpus {
15	  gpFreq: Map<string, Map<string, number>>;
16	  maxFreqPerPhoneme: Map<string, number>;
17	  knownMultiLetter: Set<string>;
18	}
19	
20	const buildCorpus = (): Corpus => {
21	  const gpFreq = new Map<string, Map<string, number>>();
22	  const maxFreqPerPhoneme = new Map<string, number>();
23	  const knownMultiLetter = new Set<string>();
24	
25	  for (const loader of Object.values(ausLoaders)) {
26	    for (const entry of loader.default) {
27	      for (const g of entry.graphemes) {
28	        const key = g.g;
29	        const inner = gpFreq.get(key) ?? new Map<string, number>();
30	        const next = (inner.get(g.p) ?? 0) + 1;
31	        inner.set(g.p, next);
32	        gpFreq.set(key, inner);
33	
34	        maxFreqPerPhoneme.set(
35	          g.p,
36	          Math.max(maxFreqPerPhoneme.get(g.p) ?? 0, next),
37	        );
38	
39	        if (g.g.length > 1 && !g.g.includes('_')) {
40	          knownMultiLetter.add(g.g);
41	        }
42	      }
43	    }
44	  }
45	  return { gpFreq, maxFreqPerPhoneme, knownMultiLetter };
46	};
47	
48	let corpus: Corpus | null = null;
49	const getCorpus = (): Corpus => {
50	  if (!corpus) corpus = buildCorpus();
51	  return corpus;
52	};
53	
54	interface Candidate {
55	  g: string;
56	  consume: number;
57	  span?: [number, number];
58	  isSplitDigraph?: boolean;
59	}
60	
61	const enumerateCandidates = (
62	  word: string,
63	  letterIdx: number,
64	  claimed: Set<number>,
65	  known: Set<string>,
66	): Candidate[] => {
67	  const results: Candidate[] = [];
68	  const remaining = word.length - letterIdx;
69	
70	  // Multi-letter graphemes from corpus (skip claimed positions)
71	  const maxLen = Math.min(4, remaining);
72	  for (let len = maxLen; len >= 2; len -= 1) {
73	    let hasClaimed = false;
74	    for (let i = 1; i < len; i += 1) {
75	      if (claimed.has(letterIdx + i)) {
76	        hasClaimed = true;
77	        break;
78	      }
79	    }
80	    if (hasClaimed) continue;
81	    const g = word.slice(letterIdx, letterIdx + len);
82	    if (known.has(g)) results.push({ g, consume: len });
83	  }
84	
85	  // Doubled consonant: always offer when next letter is identical
86	  if (
87	    remaining >= 2 &&
88	    word[letterIdx] === word[letterIdx + 1] &&
89	    !claimed.has(letterIdx + 1)
90	  ) {
91	    // Both indices are guarded by `remaining >= 2`, so they are non-null.
92	    const doubled = word[letterIdx]! + word[letterIdx + 1]!;
93	    // Only add if not already added via knownMultiLetter above
94	    if (!results.some((r) => r.g === doubled)) {
95	      results.push({ g: doubled, consume: 2 });
96	    }
97	  }
98	
99	  // Single letter fallback (always available, skip claimed)
100	  // letterIdx is always < word.length here (caller guards it).
101	  results.push({ g: word[letterIdx]!, consume: 1 });
102	
103	  // Split digraphs (consume only the leading letter; trailing is claimed)
104	  for (const digraph of SPLIT_DIGRAPHS) {
105	    const [leading, trailing] = digraph.split('_');
106	    if (word[letterIdx] !== leading) continue;
107	    const gapMin = 1;
108	    const gapMax = 2;
109	    for (let gap = gapMin; gap <= gapMax; gap += 1) {
110	      const trailIdx = letterIdx + 1 + gap;
111	      if (trailIdx >= word.length) continue;
112	      if (claimed.has(trailIdx)) continue;
113	      if (word[trailIdx] === trailing) {
114	        results.push({
115	          g: digraph,
116	          consume: 1,
117	          // span uses [leadingIdx, trailingIdx] — both inclusive indices
118	          span: [letterIdx, trailIdx],
119	          isSplitDigraph: true,
120	        });
121	      }
122	    }
123	  }
124	
125	  return results;
126	};
127	
128	const scoreCandidate = (
129	  candidate: Candidate,
130	  phoneme: string,
131	  { gpFreq, maxFreqPerPhoneme }: Corpus,
132	): number => {
133	  const inner = gpFreq.get(candidate.g);
134	  const count = inner?.get(phoneme) ?? 0;
135	  if (count > 0) {
136	    const max = maxFreqPerPhoneme.get(phoneme) ?? 1;
137	    return count / max;
138	  }
139	  // For a doubled consonant (e.g. "tt"), fall back to the single-letter score.
140	  // candidate.g.length === 2 guarantees [0] is non-null.
141	  if (candidate.g.length === 2 && candidate.g[0] === candidate.g[1]) {
142	    const singleInner = gpFreq.get(candidate.g[0]!);
143	    const singleCount = singleInner?.get(phoneme) ?? 0;
144	    if (singleCount > 0) {
145	      const max = maxFreqPerPhoneme.get(phoneme) ?? 1;
146	      return singleCount / max;
147	    }
148	  }
149	  return 0;
150	};
151	
152	const nextUnclaimedIdx = (
153	  idx: number,
154	  claimed: Set<number>,
155	): number => {
156	  let i = idx;
157	  while (claimed.has(i)) i += 1;
158	  return i;
159	};
160	
161	export const align = (
162	  word: string,
163	  phonemes: string[],
164	): AlignedGrapheme[] => {
165	  const c = getCorpus();
166	  const claimed = new Set<number>();
167	  const out: AlignedGrapheme[] = [];
168	  let letterIdx = 0;
169	  let phonemeIdx = 0;
170	
171	  while (letterIdx < word.length && phonemeIdx < phonemes.length) {
172	    letterIdx = nextUnclaimedIdx(letterIdx, claimed);
173	    if (letterIdx >= word.length) break;
174	
175	    // phonemeIdx < phonemes.length is guarded by the while condition above.
176	    const phoneme = phonemes[phonemeIdx]!;
177	    const candidates = enumerateCandidates(
178	      word,
179	      letterIdx,
180	      claimed,
181	      c.knownMultiLetter,
182	    );
183	
184	    // Compute remaining letters (excluding claimed) and remaining phonemes
185	    let lettersLeft = 0;
186	    for (let i = letterIdx; i < word.length; i += 1) {
187	      if (!claimed.has(i)) lettersLeft += 1;
188	    }
189	    const phonemesLeft = phonemes.length - phonemeIdx;
190	    const needsLonger = lettersLeft > phonemesLeft;
191	
192	    // enumerateCandidates always pushes a single-letter fallback, so
193	    // candidates is never empty and at(-1) is guaranteed non-null.
194	    let best: Candidate = candidates.at(-1)!;
195	    let bestScore = scoreCandidate(best, phoneme, c);
196	
197	    for (const cand of candidates) {
198	      const score = scoreCandidate(cand, phoneme, c);
199	      // When more letters than phonemes remain, prefer longer graphemes on tie
200	      const isBetter =
201	        needsLonger && cand.consume > best.consume
202	          ? score >= bestScore
203	          : score > bestScore;
204	      if (isBetter) {
205	        best = cand;
206	        bestScore = score;
207	      }
208	    }
209	
210	    const confidence = bestScore > 0 ? Math.min(bestScore, 1) : 0.2;
211	
212	    out.push({
213	      g: best.g,
214	      p: phoneme,
215	      span: best.span,
216	      confidence,
217	    });
218	
219	    if (best.isSplitDigraph && best.span) {
220	      // Claim the trailing letter index
221	      claimed.add(best.span[1]);
222	      letterIdx = letterIdx + best.consume;
223	    } else {
224	      letterIdx = letterIdx + best.consume;
225	    }
226	    phonemeIdx += 1;
227	  }
228	
229	  // Absorb any remaining unclaimed letters into the last grapheme
230	  if (out.length > 0) {
231	    const remaining: string[] = [];
232	    for (let i = letterIdx; i < word.length; i += 1) {
233	      // i < word.length guarantees word[i] is defined.
234	      if (!claimed.has(i)) remaining.push(word[i]!);
235	    }
236	    if (remaining.length > 0) {
237	      // out.length > 0 guarantees at(-1) is non-null.
238	      const last = out.at(-1)!;
239	      out[out.length - 1] = {
240	        ...last,
241	        g: `${last.g}${remaining.join('')}`,
242	      };
243	    }
244	  }
245	
246	  return out;
247	};
248	
249	export const __resetCorpusForTests = (): void => {
250	  corpus = null;
251	};
252
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-24 03:33:06Z)**_

The `o_e` chip is a split-digraph that claims the `e` at a later position non-contiguously — so the invariant `chips.map(c => c.g).join('') === word` **breaks** for these chips. That also breaks our split/delete logic on any word with `like`/`take`/etc. The user's "missing e" is the visible symptom.

Let me finish the IPA tokenizer (quick), then address the split-digraph issue as a separate commit.

Commit flatten + IPA-align

```bash
git add src/data/words/authoring/aligner.ts src/data/words/authoring/aligner.test.ts src/data/words/authoring/ipa.ts src/data/words/authoring/ipa.test.ts src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.tsx src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.test.tsx && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(word-authoring): flatten split-digraphs + add IPA→chips align button

Two fixes that share the "chips should always be a linear, literal
slice of the word" invariant:

1. The aligner has always emitted split-digraph chips (e.g. `a_e`
   for "cake", `o_e` for "prothesis") where `.g` is a symbolic
   notation with an underscore and `.span` points at a
   non-contiguous trailing letter. That broke the invariant
   `chips.map(c => c.g).join('') === word` — for "prothesis" the
   chip row looked like `p r o_e th s i s`, which missed the
   visible `e`, and Split/Delete/Extend/Shrink all silently
   corrupted state because they treat `.g` as a substring.

   New `flattenAlignedChips(word, chips)` helper expands every
   split-digraph chip into a leading-letter chip with the original
   phoneme plus a silent-e chip (empty phoneme, low confidence) at
   the correct trailing position. The authoring panel calls it on
   every alignment, so the chip row always satisfies the invariant
   and the "e" reappears in "prothesis".

   Kept `align` unchanged — the raw output is still useful to any
   caller that cares about the teaching semantics of split-digraphs.
   Tests: contiguous passthrough, `cake` expansion to
   ["c","a","k","e"] with silent e, and a synthetic prothesis-like
   case asserting the `e` chip is present and join matches.

2. Users with a canonical IPA (from a dictionary, say `pʊtɪŋ` for
   putting or `prɒθɪsɪs` for prothesis) had no way to re-align the
   chip phonemes without picking each one from the dropdown.

   New `tokenizeIpa(value, inventory)` helper does greedy
   longest-match over the phoneme inventory so multi-char phonemes
   (`tʃ`, `eɪ`, `iː`) bind before their prefixes. Unknown chars are
   dropped silently after `normalizeIpa` strips slashes / whitespace
   / stress.

   Inline `→ Align chips` button next to the IPA label runs
   `flattenAlignedChips(word, align(word, tokenizeIpa(ipa, …)))` and
   writes the result into chip state. Disabled when IPA normalises
   to empty or there are no chips to align. Because the label is no
   longer a simple `<label>IPA<input/></label>` (the button is
   inline with the label text), the field uses explicit `htmlFor` +
   `id` to preserve the label→input association; the
   `pre-fills IPA` test is anchored to `/^IPA$/` to disambiguate
   from the button's `Align chips from IPA` accessible name.

Six unit tests for `tokenizeIpa` covering single-char, greedy
multi-char, normalisation, unknown-char skip, and empty inputs.
Integration test types `/ˈpʊtɪŋ/` into the IPA field, clicks Align
chips, and asserts the `tt` chip retains `/t/` and chips still
join to `putting`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -6
```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-24 03:41:43Z)**_

Three commits in this round, 16 ahead of origin:

- **`c1000945`** — engine now uses `RiTa.analyze` + LTS; `prothesis` gets guessed phonemes/syllables; banner re-worded to "letter-to-sound guesses, please confirm in dictionary".
- **`7bc78366`** — syllables are stateful. Editable just like chips: bootstrap for unknowns, ✂ Split, 🗑 Delete, selection ring, join-equals-word invariant.
- **`43c23df9`** — two related fixes: split-digraph chips (`o_e`, `a_e`) are flattened so every chip is a literal substring and the `e` in `prothesis` is visible; new **→ Align chips** button next to the IPA label re-runs the aligner using tokenised IPA (greedy longest-match over 45 phonemes).

**What to verify:**

1. Type `prothesis` → chips: `p r o t h e s i s` (9 chips, **`e` now visible**) + amber banner warning about LTS guesses + syllables the user can split.
2. Paste `/ˈpʊtɪŋ/` in a word's IPA field → click **→ Align chips** → chip phonemes re-align to `/p/ /ʊ/ /t/ /ɪ/ /ŋ/`. Join still equals the word.
3. Click any syllable → ✂ Split / 🗑 Delete; invariant `syllables.join('') === word` holds.
4. `cake` (if rita knows it) → chips: `c a k e` with the silent `e` as its own (empty-phoneme) chip, not hidden inside an `a_e`.

Want me to keep going on the remaining handoff items (deferred PR #182 list: VR baselines, Dexie resolutions, CLI semantics, mobile entry points, etc.) or take a break here?

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-24 03:46:20Z)**_

Got it — 5 buttons. Clarifying:

- **Move left**: shift one letter from current chip → previous chip (both chips remain, count unchanged).
- **Join left**: merge entire current chip into previous chip (count shrinks by 1).
- **Split**: divide current chip into two (count grows by 1).
- **Join right**: merge current chip into next chip.
- **Move right**: shift one letter from current chip → next chip.

And for syllables I'll do the simpler 3-button variant (Join-L / Split / Join-R) — letters-within-syllable rebalancing is less useful.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-24 03:51:28Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Commit five-button editor

```bash
git add src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.tsx src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.test.tsx && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(word-authoring): chip + syllable editor get five directional buttons

The old chip editor offered «−» / «+» / «✂ Split» / «🗑 Delete». Once a
user split every chip down to a single letter (common for rita-unknown
words like "prothesis" or "surprise"), shrink and extend both stalled
— they can't move a letter out of a single-letter chip — and Delete
only ever merged in one default direction. So there was no way to
rejoin two single-letter chips you'd intentionally split apart.

Replace with five buttons operating on the currently-selected chip:

- ‹ Move      — move the first letter to the previous chip
- « Join      — merge this chip into the previous chip
- ✂ Split    — split this chip at the last letter (unchanged)
- Join »     — merge this chip into the next chip
- Move ›     — move the last letter to the next chip

The split between Move and Join matters: Move preserves the chip
count and rebalances the boundary between two adjacent chips; Join
removes the current chip and rolls its letters into a neighbour.
Move still requires a multi-letter source, but Join works on any
single-letter chip, closing the gap the user hit.

Disabled rules:
- `‹ Move` / `« Join`   disabled on the first chip (no left
  neighbour); `‹ Move` also when the chip has a single letter.
- `✂ Split`             disabled when the chip has a single letter.
- `Join »` / `Move ›`   disabled on the last chip; `Move ›` also
  when the chip has a single letter.
- Both `Join` buttons disabled when there's only one chip.

Syllables get the same five-button treatment (same helpers renamed
accordingly). The hint line above each row now reflects the four
actions.

The save path is unchanged — still persists chips / syllables as-is.

TDD: rewrote the three old delete-based chip tests into direction-
specific join tests, added move-letter-left for chips and move-
letter-right for syllables, plus a "first chip disables both left
operations" test. Removed the obsolete shrink/extend/delete helpers.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5
```

```text

Done in 18.81s.
```

Committed locally as `d845d0ae`. 17 ahead of origin, still no push.

**Button row (chips and syllables both):**

| Label   | Effect                                           | Disabled when                           |
|---------|--------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| ‹ Move  | first letter → previous chip                     | first chip, or chip has 1 letter        |
| « Join  | merge current → previous chip                    | first chip, or only one chip            |
| ✂ Split | split current at last letter                     | chip has 1 letter                       |
| Join »  | merge current → next chip                        | last chip, or only one chip             |
| Move ›  | last letter → next chip                          | last chip, or chip has 1 letter         |

**Unblocks your scenarios:**
- `prothesis` / `surprise` / `aluminium` / `aluminum` — after splitting down to single letters, click any chip → `« Join` or `Join »` merges adjacent single-letter chips back.
- Still rebalance multi-letter chips with `‹ Move` / `Move ›` exactly like the old `−`/`+` did, just from the current chip's perspective.

```bash
git add src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.tsx src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.test.tsx && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(word-authoring): surface three word-reference links under the Word input

Whenever the Word field has any text, the authoring panel now exposes
a small row of three external lookup links directly beneath the
input, so the author can cross-check the word against public
references in one tap:

- Dictionary.com   — https://www.dictionary.com/browse/<word>
- Vocabulary.com   — https://www.vocabulary.com/dictionary/<word>
- HowManySyllables — https://www.howmanysyllables.com/syllables/<word>

Useful for rita-known words too (verify stress, alternate
pronunciations), not just LTS-guessed ones, so the row is always
visible when a word is present. The amber banner's existing
dictionary.com link stays — it's still the right CTA in the
"we guessed this" context — but the new always-visible row means
authors don't have to type the word into a new tab's URL bar.

aria-labels are "Look up <word> on <site>" so screen readers
announce the target word and destination.

TDD: failing test asserts all three links exist with correct
hrefs for `initialWord="putting"`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5
```

Commit B

```bash
git add src/data/words/WordLibraryExplorer.tsx src/data/words/WordLibraryExplorer.authoring.test.tsx src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.tsx src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.test.tsx src/data/words/filter.ts src/data/words/filter.drafts.test.ts && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(word-authoring): edit shipped entries as draft overrides

Authors couldn't correct mistakes in shipped words — the Edit affordance
only existed in the Drafts panel, and the authoring flow blocked any
word that collided with shipped data. Fix that end-to-end without
mutating the curriculum JSON at runtime: a shipped Edit creates a
draft that shadows the shipped entry; the CLI (`yarn words:import`)
already knows how to promote drafts back into the JSON.

- `ResultCard` gains an optional `onEdit` prop and renders an "Edit"
  button in the header when it's provided. Applies to both shipped
  and draft results so the same affordance covers both.
- `WordLibraryExplorer.handleEditHit` decides the seed: for a draft
  hit it looks up the live DraftEntry from the store; for a shipped
  hit it synthesises a DraftEntry shape with `id: ''` (the "not
  persisted" sentinel) pre-filled from the hit's word/ipa/level/
  graphemes/syllables.
- `AuthoringPanel.handleSave` routes on `initialDraft.id` truthiness:
  empty id → `draftStore.saveDraft` (creates a new draft row);
  truthy id → `draftStore.updateDraft` (existing behaviour).
- The shipped-collision check is only a save-blocker for the
  "new-from-scratch" flow (`initialDraft === undefined`). When
  editing a shipped entry the panel swaps the red "already exists"
  warning for an amber info line explaining that Save will create a
  shadowing draft that doesn't touch the curriculum JSON.
- `filter.ts` now dedupes shipped + drafts by word: whenever a draft
  exists for the same word as a shipped entry, the draft is the
  authoritative result. Without this, the grid would show "an"
  twice after an override. Applied to both the primary region path
  and the fallback-to-AUS path.

TDD: filter test creates a same-word draft and asserts the shipped
entry drops out of the hits; panel test uses an empty-id seed,
checks the override banner, and verifies Save persists a fresh
draft with a generated id; explorer test clicks Edit on a shipped
Level-1 card ("an") and asserts the override banner renders.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5
```

Base directory for this skill: /Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/.claude/skills/handoff

# Handoff

Adapted from the [Cult of Claude handoff skill](https://cultofclaude.com/skills/handoff/) ([source](https://github.com/robertguss/claude-code-toolkit/blob/main/skills/handoff/SKILL.md)), tailored for BaseSkill's worktree + `/resync` + baby-step commit workflow.

## When to use

- Just saved a plan or spec (→ next session executes it)
- Just raised or merged a PR (→ next session starts follow-up work)
- Ending a work session or context-switching
- User says "hand this off", "continue in a new session", "pick this up later"

Explicit invocation: `/handoff` or `/continue`.

## Worktree rule

Handoffs are written inside the **active worktree**, never on `master`. If the current working directory is not under `worktrees/<name>/`, stop and ask the user whether to create a worktree first (see [CLAUDE.md](../../../CLAUDE.md) worktree gate).

## Process

### Step 1 — Auto-detect context

Run these commands and keep the output for the template:

```bash
git branch --show-current
git worktree list | grep "$(pwd)"
git status --porcelain | wc -l
git rev-list --count @{u}..HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo 0
git log -1 --pretty=format:"%h %s"
gh pr view --json number,state,url 2>/dev/null || true
```

Also scan `docs/superpowers/specs/` and `docs/superpowers/plans/` for recently-modified files whose slug matches the current branch or task (optional — include only if clearly relevant).

### Step 2 — Ask one question

> "Anything specific you want captured? (key decisions, gotchas, things you'll forget)"

Single prompt, no follow-up loop. The user can answer briefly or skip.

### Step 3 — Generate the document

Fill in this template. **Omit any section with no content** — keep the document scannable.

````markdown
# Handoff: <brief description>

**Date:** YYYY-MM-DD
**Branch:** <branch-name>
**Worktree:** worktrees/<name>
**Worktree path:** <absolute path>
**Git status:** clean | N uncommitted | N unpushed commits ahead of origin
**Last commit:** <sha> <subject>
**PR:** #NNN — open|merged|closed (omit if none)

## Resume command

```
/resync
cd worktrees/<name>
<next action — e.g., execute plan docs/…md, open PR, resume debugging>
```

## Current state

**Task:** <what we're working on>
**Phase:** exploration | planning | implementation | debugging | review | shipped
**Progress:** <milestone or %>

## What we did

<2–3 sentences>

## Decisions made

- **<decision>** — <why>

## Spec / Plan

- docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md
- docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>.md

## Key files

- src/…/foo.ts:42 — <what and why>

## Open questions / blockers

- [ ] <question>

## Next steps

1. [ ] <first action>
2. [ ] <second>

## Context to remember

<constraints, user preferences, domain knowledge not obvious from the code>
````

### Step 4 — Write the file

Save to `.claude/handoffs/YYYY-MM-DD-<brief-slug>.md`. Slug is 2–5 lowercase words joined by hyphens, matching the branch topic.

### Step 5 — Commit as a baby step

```bash
git add .claude/handoffs/YYYY-MM-DD-<slug>.md
git commit -m "docs(handoff): <brief description>"
```

### Step 6 — Print the resume hint

Tell the user:

> Saved handoff: `.claude/handoffs/YYYY-MM-DD-<slug>.md` — paste that path in a new session to resume.

## Quality check (before writing)

Mental pass — no validation script:

1. Could a fresh Claude (or Cursor) pick up from this?
2. Are decisions traceable (the "why", not just the "what")?
3. Are next steps actionable and specific?
4. Are file paths anchored (`foo.ts:42`, not "that function")?

If any answer is "no", tighten the relevant section before saving.

## What to capture

### Always

- Decisions with reasoning — the "why"
- File paths touched, with intent
- Current progress — where we stopped
- Actionable next steps
- User context — constraints, preferences, domain knowledge

### When relevant

- Errors hit and how they resolved (or didn't)
- Dead ends — approaches tried that didn't work (saves re-exploration)

### Skip

- Verbose tool output
- Intermediate reasoning that reached conclusions
- Information obvious from the code

**Date:** 2026-04-24

Last session added the full authoring surface (chips/syllables editors with 5 directional buttons, IPA→chip align, shipped override, reference links, phoneme inventory fix, play button, LTS fallback via `RiTa.analyze`). Two small UX bugs remain.

## What we did in this session (summary)

18 commits addressing every UX gap from the previous handoff plus several that surfaced during manual testing. The stack (newest first):

| Commit      | Summary                                                                 |
| ----------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `e9cba303`  | Edit shipped entries as draft overrides (ResultCard Edit, filter dedup) |

Also raised one issue: [#186](https://github.com/leocaseiro/base-skill/issues/186) — compose compound words from existing parts (sun + day → sunday).

## Decisions made (with reasoning)

- **`RiTa.analyze` replaces `hasWord`-gated `phones`/`syllables`.** Analyze works for any word (LTS fallback for dictionary misses); `hasWord` is kept purely to drive the amber "this is a guess" banner.
- **Split-digraphs flattened in `AuthoringPanel` post-alignment.** `aligner.ts` still emits `a_e` / `o_e` symbolic chips with non-contiguous `span`, but the new `flattenAlignedChips(word, chips)` helper expands them into literal letters + a silent-e chip so `chips.map(c => c.g).join('') === word` holds. Keeps the aligner's teaching semantics intact for other callers.
- **Five directional buttons over the old `− + Delete`.** Move / Join separate letter-boundary rebalancing from chip-count changes, which closes the single-letter-chip merge gap. Syllables use the same five buttons.
- **Shipped edits land as drafts with `id: ''` seeds, not in-place JSON mutations.** The filter dedupes so a shipped word with a same-word draft shows exactly once (draft wins). Promotes via the existing `yarn words:import` CLI. Curriculum JSON is never mutated at runtime.
- **`ipa.ts` strips slashes, whitespace, and stress marks** (`ˈ` / `ˌ`) on save and render; length marks (`ː`) pass through. Matches zero instances of slash/space/stress in shipped AUS JSON (length marks present). Also exposes `tokenizeIpa` (greedy longest-match over a phoneme inventory).

## Spec / Plan

- Prior handoff: `.claude/handoffs/2026-04-24-word-authoring-pr182-ux-gaps.md`
- Design spec: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-23-word-authoring-design.md`
- Plan (largely superseded by the commits above): `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-24-word-authoring-implementation.md`

## Key files

- `src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.tsx` — form state (`useDebouncedBreakdown`), chip + syllable editors, save path (routes on `initialDraft.id` truthiness), shipped-override banner
- `src/data/words/authoring/aligner.ts` — `align` (unchanged) + `flattenAlignedChips` (new, expands split-digraphs)
- `src/data/words/authoring/engine.ts` — `RiTa.analyze` entry point, catches LTS parse errors
- `src/data/words/authoring/ipa.ts` — `normalizeIpa`, `tokenizeIpa`
- `src/data/words/filter.ts` — `loadShippedWordLevels`, `isLevelInFilter`, draft-shadows-shipped dedupe in `filterWords`
- `src/data/words/WordLibraryExplorer.tsx` — `handleEditHit` synthesises the draft seed (`id: ''` for shipped, live DraftEntry lookup for drafts); `ResultCard` now takes an `onEdit` prop; grid renders each word once thanks to filter dedupe

## Next steps (in suggested order)

1. [ ] **Bug 1: Stale list after save/delete in the Drafts panel.** After saving / updating / deleting a draft, the `WordLibraryExplorer` grid and the Drafts panel list don't refresh — user has to reload the page.
      - **Likely cause:** the `filterWords` effect in `WordLibraryExplorer.tsx:617` depends on `[filter]` only. When `authoringWord` / `editingDraft` transitions to `null` on save, we refresh `draftCount` (line 610) but not the result grid. The `DraftsPanel` has its own `refresh` that fires on `[open]` (see `src/data/words/authoring/DraftsPanel.tsx:54`) so it only updates when the panel is re-opened.
      - **Simplest fix:** add a `draftVersion: number` state in `WordLibraryExplorer` that bumps on every save/delete (pass a setter or a callback down). Include it in the `filterWords` effect's deps. Pass a similar callback into `DraftsPanel` so it re-refreshes after its own delete. TDD: write a failing test that renders the explorer, saves a draft via the panel, closes the panel, and asserts the new draft appears in the grid without a re-render.
      - **Alternative:** broadcast via a tiny Dexie `liveQuery` or a hook on `draftStore` that emits on mutations. Heavier, but decouples the two views.
2. [ ] **Bug 2: Word field shouldn't be editable in edit mode; add a "Re-generate from RitaJS" button.** User's phrasing: _"In Edit mode, we can't change the word, so we can make the field disable. With that, we should instead, have a button to re-generate from RitaJS. It should be optional when the field is no longer empty after the first blur."_
      - **Intent (my read — confirm with user if unsure):**
        - In edit mode (`initialDraft` is set, either truthy `id` for an existing draft or `''` for a shipped override) → the Word input is disabled; a Re-generate button is always visible.
        - In new-word mode (`initialDraft === undefined`) → the Word input stays editable while the user is composing. Once the field is non-empty AND has been blurred once, disable it and show the Re-generate button. The button is optional ("optional" = the user can choose to press it or keep their manual edits; it's not required to make progress).
      - **Why disable the Word in edit mode:** editing the word itself is effectively a rename, which `draftStore` doesn't support (unique `[region+word]` index, and no migration for the row). Safer to force the user to cancel and re-create.
      - **Re-generate button behaviour:** calls `generateBreakdown(word)` again (same path as the debounced first run), then re-seeds chips / IPA / syllables / level from the result. Prompt to confirm when the form is dirty so the user doesn't silently lose edits.
      - **TDD:** (a) edit-mode test: rendering with `initialDraft` makes the Word input have `disabled`; (b) new-word test: type a word, don't blur, input stays enabled; blur once while non-empty, input becomes disabled and a "Re-generate" button appears; (c) clicking Re-generate invokes `generateBreakdown` and updates the chips/syllables (easiest to assert via the mock engine in `AuthoringPanel.test.tsx`).
      - **Location:** `src/data/words/authoring/AuthoringPanel.tsx` — Word input around the `{word.trim() && (...)}` reference-links block (currently around line 596). Add an `onBlur` handler on the input that flips a `wordCommitted` boolean once `word.trim() !== ''`. For edit mode, treat `wordCommitted` as initially `true`.

## Context to remember

- **User preferences (memory entries):**
  - Named exports only (no `export default` except framework configs).
  - TDD required for every bug fix — failing test first, commit includes the test.
  - Commit freely; push only with explicit confirmation (bug-fix flow).

- **Word is an immutable key for drafts.** The `[region+word]` unique index in `draftStore` (`src/data/words/authoring/draftStore.ts:15`) means changing `word` on an existing draft would require a delete + save pair, which breaks referential integrity with any consumers that pinned `draftId`. Hence Bug 2's "disable word in edit mode" framing.
- **Shipped-override flow is working end-to-end.** Any code you add must not break the `initialDraft.id === ''` discriminator — `WordLibraryExplorer.handleEditHit` synthesises this for shipped hits, and `AuthoringPanel.handleSave` routes on the same check.
- **`flattenAlignedChips` is required on every alignment site.** If you add a new path that calls `align(word, phonemes)` (e.g. IPA re-align → yes, already does), flatten before setting chips, or the grapheme-chip invariant breaks and Split/Join misbehave silently.
- **Pre-commit hook is bucket-gated** (`scripts/detect-buckets.mjs`). A single file edit may not trigger typecheck/lint. Always `yarn lint && yarn typecheck` locally before a commit you intend to push.
